title: PRISM
text:
PRISM is a comprehensive machine learning toolkit designed to advance auditory scene analysis (Ecological Momentary Assessment-EMA) for hearing devices. This repository contains the code, models, and tools developed for the “Australian Future Hearing Initiative”.
PRISM might stand for: * PRedicting Interaction difficulty using Sensors and Micro-EMA” * Personalized Real-time Interaction difficulty for Sounds Measurement * Platform for Research In Situational Momentary assessment.

title: PRISM
text:
PRISM
Modern hearing aids often struggle to adapt to rapidly changing sound environments—like moving from a quiet room to a busy street or a noisy restaurant. This technology bridges the gap between raw audio processing and intelligent environmental awareness, leading to a more seamless and personalised hearing experience for users.

What is “Ecological Momentary Assessment” (EMA)?
EMA is used to “catch you in the moment.” When you interact with the PRISM app, we capture a snapshot of your real-world environment. This includes: - Audio: A short 30-second clip of the sound around you. - Context: Your rating of how hard it was to hear speech.
This data trains a Machine Learning models to automatically recognize difficult listening situations and adjust hearing aids in real-time.


Key Resources

Paper: A dataset and model for auditory scene recognition for hearing devices: AHEAD-DS and OpenYAMNet (arXiv:2508.10360)
Datasets:

AHEAD-DS (HuggingFace): The primary dataset for training and benchmarking.
AHEAD-DS Unmixed (HuggingFace): Component audio files before mixing, useful for custom augmentation strategies.

Models:

OpenYAMNet Models (HuggingFace): Pre-trained models ready for inference or fine-tuning.

title: PRISM
text:
Capabilities

Benchmark New Models: Use the standardized ahead_ds split to compare your model’s performance against the OpenYAMNet baseline.
Fine-Tuning: Leverage pre-trained YAMNet+ weights to adapt the model to new acoustic domains with minimal data.
Reproducibility: Full scripts for dataset generation (prism-ml-dataset-ahead-ds) and model training (prism-ml-yamnetp-tune) are provided.

title: PRISM
text:
Citation
If you use this code, model, or dataset in your research, please cite:
@misc{zhong2026datasetmodelauditoryscene,
      title={A dataset and model for auditory scene recognition for hearing devices: AHEAD-DS and OpenYAMNet}, 
      author={Henry Zhong and Jörg M. Buchholz and Julian Maclaren and Simon Carlile and Richard Lyon},
      year={2026},
      eprint={2508.10360},
      archivePrefix={arXiv},
      primaryClass={cs.SD},
      url={https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.10360}, 
}

title: PRISM
section: Setting up this repo for auto-building the github pages site
text:
Setting up this repo for auto-building the github pages site
FYI, this only has to be done once. And has already been done. Now just fork this repo or use it as a template when making a new page!
Build the workshop files locally with
git clone  
cd prism-docs
quarto render index.qmd 
git commit -am "New build"
git push
You must also make a gh-pages branch and publish the quarto pages to it locally first. Probably better ways to do this:
git checkout -b gh-pages
quarto publish gh-pages
git push origin gh-pages 
git checkout main
On the repo turn on github pages at: https://github.com/Australian-Future-Hearing-Initiative/prism-docs/settings/pages And have it render from gh-pages branch.
Add this github action file to render the quarto page on any repo updates. Note any code execution (e.g Python or executable blocks with {} syntax will need more installations).
name: Quarto Publish

on:
  push:
    branches: main

permissions:
  contents: write
  
jobs:
  build-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check out repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Set up Quarto
        uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2
        with:
          tinytex: true

      - name: Publish to GitHub Pages (and render)
        uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/publish@v2
        with:
          target: gh-pages
          path: .
